{"lessons":[{"lessonId":"lesson-6","facts":["Metamorphism changes a preexisting rock without melting it completely.","Heat, pressure, and chemically active fluids drive metamorphic reactions.","Foliation forms when platy minerals align under directed stress.","Parent rock composition strongly influences the metamorphic rock produced.","Grade refers to the intensity of metamorphism, not rock quality."],"keyTerms":[{"tr":"metamorphism","df":"The solid-state alteration of a preexisting rock through changes in temperature, pressure, and fluid chemistry. The original rock is transformed mineralogically and texturally without wholesale melting.","eg":"Shale transformed into slate during low-grade metamorphism.","mi":"Metamorphism means the rock has melted and recrystallized as magma.","cx":"Metamorphism occurs below melting, so the rock remains solid while minerals and texture change."},{"tr":"foliation","df":"A planar fabric in metamorphic rock caused by the preferred alignment of minerals or compositional banding under directed stress.","eg":"Schist commonly shows visible alignment of mica flakes.","mi":"Any layered-looking rock is foliated.","cx":"True foliation is produced by metamorphic deformation and mineral alignment, not by simple sedimentary layering."},{"tr":"parent rock","df":"The original rock that undergoes metamorphism, also called the protolith.","eg":"Limestone is the parent rock for marble.","mi":"The parent rock does not matter because metamorphism always makes the same product.","cx":"The protolith controls which minerals are available, so different parent rocks can yield different metamorphic products."}],"scenario":{"su":"A roadcut exposes two nearby rocks beside an igneous intrusion. Rock A is coarse-grained and nonfoliated. Rock B is shiny and shows visible mineral alignment. Both rocks formed from different parent materials at the same time.","ma":"Field descriptions of texture, proximity to intrusion, and inferred parent rocks."},"discussionPrompt":{"pr":"Should texture or mineral assemblage be used first when inferring metamorphic conditions from a hand sample?","tn":"Reasonable positions differ because texture reflects deformation history, while minerals more directly reflect pressure-temperature conditions.","po":["Texture first, because foliation and grain size are the quickest clues in the field.","Minerals first, because index minerals better constrain metamorphic grade.","Use both together, because neither texture nor minerals alone gives the full history."]},"assignmentCore":{"td":"Analyze a set of hand-sample descriptions and identify which rocks are foliated, which are nonfoliated, and what parent rock or metamorphic setting each likely records. Then explain the reasoning using metamorphic grade, stress direction, and parent-rock composition.","pa":["Use 4-6 sample descriptions.","Limit each explanation to 2-3 sentences.","Do not use melting or igneous classification language.","Support each identification with at least one texture clue and one composition clue."]},"mc":[{"q":"Which process best defines metamorphism?","op":["Solid-state change of a preexisting rock","Complete melting of a rock into magma","Weathering and erosion at Earth’s surface","Deposition of sediments in layers"],"ai":0,"ex":"Metamorphism alters an existing rock in the solid state, typically by heat, pressure, and fluids, without complete melting.","fi":[0,1]},{"q":"A shale exposed to increasing pressure and temperature commonly becomes which metamorphic rock?","op":["Slate","Basalt","Pumice","Conglomerate"],"ai":0,"ex":"Shale commonly metamorphoses into slate at low grade, where fine-grained minerals align to produce foliation.","fi":[1,2]},{"q":"A rock with parallel alignment of mica minerals most directly indicates what metamorphic feature?","op":["Foliation","Vesicular texture","Sedimentary cross-bedding","Glassy texture"],"ai":0,"ex":"Aligned platy minerals produce foliation, a planar metamorphic fabric formed by directed stress.","fi":[2]},{"q":"Which statement about contact metamorphism is most accurate?","op":["It commonly produces nonfoliated rocks near igneous intrusions","It always produces foliated rocks from strong shear stress","It requires complete melting of the surrounding country rock","It occurs only at the seafloor under low pressure"],"ai":0,"ex":"Contact metamorphism is dominated by heat from an intrusion and often forms nonfoliated rocks because pressure is typically not strongly directed.","fi":[1,2]}],"studyGuide":{"sm":"Connect metamorphism, foliation, and parent rock by explaining these claims: Metamorphism changes a preexisting rock without melting it completely. Heat, pressure, and chemically active fluids drive metamorphic reactions.","rs":"Retrieve each definition and correction, then apply the three terms to the scenario without notes."},"workedExample":{"wp":"A shale is buried and altered at low to moderate temperature and pressure without melting. What metamorphic rock is most likely produced, and what texture is expected?","ws":["Shale is a fine-grained pelitic rock that commonly develops foliation during low-grade metamorphism.","Low-grade metamorphism of shale typically produces slate.","Slate is usually foliated and splits into thin sheets."],"wr":"Slate; foliated, fine-grained rock"}}]}

{"lessons":[{"lessonId":"lesson-1","facts":["Physical geology studies Earth materials, processes, and the systems that shape the planet.","Earth is an interacting system of the geosphere, hydrosphere, atmosphere, and biosphere.","Energy from the Sun drives weathering, erosion, and much surface change.","Internal heat drives plate tectonics, volcanism, and mountain building.","Rocks record the conditions under which they formed and changed."],"keyTerms":[{"tr":"geosphere","df":"The solid Earth, including rocks, minerals, soils, and the planet's interior. It is the physical framework on which other Earth systems interact.","eg":"A mountain range, the crust beneath a continent, and the mantle below it are parts of the geosphere.","mi":"The geosphere means only the surface rocks people can see.","cx":"The geosphere includes both surface materials and Earth's deep interior."},{"tr":"Earth system","df":"A set of interacting Earth components that exchange matter and energy. Changes in one component can influence others.","eg":"Heavy rainfall in the atmosphere can increase runoff in rivers and accelerate erosion of the geosphere.","mi":"Earth systems operate independently, each with no effect on the others.","cx":"Earth systems are linked, so change in one part can produce responses in others."},{"tr":"weathering","df":"The physical and chemical breakdown of rock at or near Earth's surface. It prepares material for transport but does not require movement by water or wind.","eg":"Freeze-thaw cracking of rock on a cliff face is weathering.","mi":"Weathering is the same as erosion.","cx":"Weathering breaks rock down in place; erosion moves the material away."}],"scenario":{"su":"A steep volcanic slope shows fresh cracks in bedrock, loose broken fragments at the base, and muddy runoff moving downslope after a storm. Nearby, a river is carrying suspended sediment toward a delta.","ma":"Slope description, storm runoff observation, and river sediment transport record"},"discussionPrompt":{"pr":"Is it more useful to think of Earth as a set of separate spheres or as one interconnected system?","tn":"Reasonable disagreement comes from whether separation helps simplify analysis or whether interactions are the most important feature.","po":["Separate spheres can help organize complex Earth processes for study.","An integrated system better explains how changes spread across components.","Both views are useful, depending on the question being asked."]},"assignmentCore":{"td":"Analyze a simple Earth-surface case in which rain, rock breakdown, and sediment transport occur together. Identify which processes are weathering, which are erosion, and which Earth spheres interact. State how energy and matter move through the situation.","pa":["200-300 words","Use the terms geosphere, hydrosphere, atmosphere, and biosphere correctly","Include at least one example of energy transfer and one example of matter movement","Complete the response within 500 words."]},"mc":[{"q":"Which term best describes the solid Earth, including rocks, soils, and the interior?","op":["Geosphere","Hydrosphere","Atmosphere","Biosphere"],"ai":0,"ex":"The geosphere includes Earth's solid materials from the surface to the interior.","fi":[0,1]},{"q":"A hillside rock breaks apart because water repeatedly freezes and expands in cracks. Which process is this?","op":["Weathering","Erosion","Deposition","Metamorphism"],"ai":0,"ex":"The rock is broken down in place, which is weathering rather than transport.","fi":[2,4]},{"q":"A river carries sediment from a mountain valley toward the ocean. Which Earth system interaction is most directly shown?","op":["Hydrosphere moving geosphere material","Atmosphere forming mantle rock","Biosphere creating ocean crust","Geosphere stopping all transport"],"ai":0,"ex":"Water in the hydrosphere is transporting material from the geosphere.","fi":[1]},{"q":"A student claims weathering and erosion mean the same thing. Which evaluation is most accurate?","op":["The claim is incorrect because weathering breaks rock down and erosion transports it","The claim is correct because both always involve rivers","The claim is correct because both happen only underground","The claim is incorrect because erosion always forms magma"],"ai":0,"ex":"Weathering is breakdown in place; erosion is removal and transport.","fi":[2,4]}],"studyGuide":{"sm":"Connect geosphere, Earth system, and weathering by explaining these claims: Physical geology studies Earth materials, processes, and the systems that shape the planet. Earth is an interacting system of the geosphere, hydrosphere, atmosphere, and biosphere.","rs":"Retrieve each definition and correction, then apply the three terms to the scenario without notes."},"workedExample":{"wp":"A stream carries 18 kg of sediment downstream in one day. If 6 kg are deposited on the floodplain, how much sediment remains in transport?","ws":["Start with 18 kg of sediment being carried.","Subtract the 6 kg deposited on the floodplain.","18 kg - 6 kg = 12 kg."],"wr":"12 kg"}}]}

{"lessons":[{"lessonId":"lesson-10","facts":["Energy balance is the relationship between energy intake and energy expenditure.","Body weight tends to increase when intake consistently exceeds expenditure.","Body weight tends to decrease when expenditure consistently exceeds intake.","Basal metabolism is the energy needed to support basic body functions at rest.","Physical activity can vary widely and strongly affects total daily energy expenditure."],"keyTerms":[{"tr":"energy balance","df":"The comparison between calories consumed and calories used by the body over time.","eg":"A person eating 2,400 kcal daily and expending about 2,400 kcal daily is roughly in energy balance.","mi":"Energy balance means weight never changes from day to day.","cx":"Short-term fluctuations can occur; energy balance is assessed over time."},{"tr":"kilocalorie (kcal)","df":"A unit of energy used on food labels and in nutrition to quantify dietary energy.","eg":"A snack label listing 250 kcal indicates the energy provided by that food.","mi":"A kcal is the same as a kilogram of body weight.","cx":"A kilocalorie measures energy, not mass."},{"tr":"basal metabolic rate","df":"The energy required to maintain vital functions at rest, such as breathing and circulation.","eg":"Even while sleeping, the body uses energy to keep organs functioning.","mi":"Basal metabolic rate is the energy used only during exercise.","cx":"It covers resting maintenance needs, not activity energy."}],"scenario":{"su":"A 28-year-old office worker eats a 500 kcal breakfast, a 700 kcal lunch, a 900 kcal dinner, and 300 kcal in snacks.
